item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dollar amount felt really low.

It was, and it's my bad for forgetting to convey that this was intentional on the plaintiff's part. They weren't after the money, and as such they chose low numbers; they just felt it necessary/a public service to make a scene and bring negative publicity to the Foundation through use of the court system (since that's one of the only places where the Foundation can't just erase what they don't want people to know).

The Foundation seemed to be taking it a bit too seriously, for what it is.

And relatedly, they're not really taking the case or the money seriously, they're taking the negative publicity seriously. But like I said, that's an element under the surface that I kind of neglected to make clear, so I'll probably make some edits when the contest is over. Thanks for pointing it out, and I'm glad you liked it overall!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like most of my critiques are about the lawsuit, but that's definitely not important.

… Fuck it, here they are anyway:

+ show block
– hide block

- For a wingnut pro se case, it definitely scans, but it might've been more fun for other folks (and still realistic) if it was crazier.
- The dollar amount felt really low.
- The Foundation seemed to be taking it a bit too seriously, for what it is.

That said, I'm absolutely no kind of expert, I've just been down some weird YouTube rabbit-holes lately.
—-

The last twist was kind of predictable and I think their plan with the reporter was pretty half-baked, but that's more them being bad people than bad writing.

None of that really matters. Most importantly, I love the character work and worldbuilding to death.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is not the easiest read. I don't think it was a good idea to confront the reader with well over a thousand words of legalese right at the outset of the tale, especially when it doesn't contain anything that isn't stated in a few sentences elsewhere. You've mentioned in this thread that some people skipped it and ended up fine, but there's no indication that it's something that could or should be skipped, and that's poor story structure.

It kind of devalues the other collapsibles a bit, too. It's the only collapsible that doesn't add very much, but it's also the first, so it sets up an expectation that other collapsibles might be superfluous or they might confront you with a wall of text, which isn't at all how the rest of the tale goes. It's not a good first impression.

The rest of the story had some compelling elements, but I can't say anything felt particularly captivating. The Lethe scenario is played down enough that it doesn't register a lot of tension, nor does the lawsuit angle, which is again fairly oblique to the main thrust of the narrative. And that main thrust isn't exceptionally strong; I'm not given much of a reason to care about what's going on with Trauss, or the site experiencing financial issues, or the ramifications of the Foundation using mind-control. I'm not really sold on anything contained in the tale, and it lacks a satisfying arc for most of its narrative threads.

Side note, not really a critique: I find it really weird when anyone in-universe comments that the Foundation shouldn't be using anomalous things to aid in containment when literally the most distinctive and vital tool they have is an anomalous drug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite possibly one of the most horrifying articles I have read on this site.
(And I just said that to myself half an hour ago about the SCP linked to forgetting that dogs exist.)

Of everything I don't like about the (in-universe) Foundation, it's that they feel the ends justify the means. You captured that in the most gut-wrenching way possible. I seriously want to kill them now, I'm that freaked out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, but those are written as informative, usually.

Except Information Breach just doesn't do that. It's not who they are. They're already established in the canon as a biased anti-Foundation source of current events. Brandon Walkers doesn't care if pro-Foundation people read his tone as uninformative, because he's a shit-stirrer anyway and doesn't mind if he comes off as antagonistic — he has the following to get away with it, because a lot of people hate the Foundation and don't blink twice at opinion takes on events relating to them.

The lead should be be unburied. Your first sentence should be the worst new thing the foundation has done.

The worst new thing the Foundation has done is hide their ownership of South Coast Paper and get sued for it, and that is what the first sentence is about, as well as the headline.

It's written like a story, not a news/propaganda article. It's a good story.

I'm thinking that you think the collapsible prose sections are supposed to be interpreted as written by the reporter. The news article is what you see without clicking anything; if you read the whole thing without clicking the collapsibles, it's a news article, and the collapsibles are just normal sentences that happen to be in red text color. If you click the collapsibles, you get a four-part tale about the same stuff the news article is about, except behind-the-scenes. This format has functioned pretty seamlessly in my other Broken Masquerade works, with this being the first time someone's held issue with the formatting, so I'm not sure what you would want changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, but those are written as informative, usually. The lead should be be unburied. Your first sentence should be the worst new thing the foundation has done.

It's written like a story, not a news/propaganda article. It's a good story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no series on this site I both love and hate so much as Information Breach. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry by "entire thing"I meant the first collapsible.

Anyway I went back and read the rest of it and it's really good, so I upvoted, but I stand by my original assessment of that first collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In-universe, Information Breach is a newsletter dedicated to exposing the secrets of the Foundation, as part of the Broken Masquerade canon. Thus, it's performing both opinion and informative roles, as it's a biased source providing info.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a newspaper article, is this opinion or informative? You've got elements of both. The buried lead was a serious style break. In-universe, who is the intended audience?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's definitely not all that happens in this narrative — it's not even half of what's going on. I had a lot of preliminary feedback on the inclusion of the legal document from people who said the format was interesting in the same way the scientific format of SCPs is interesting, which is why I kept it in there, but I also had someone skip that collapsible and do just fine understanding the rest of it.

Edit: Gotcha, that's fair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't get through the first collapsible. Maybe some people enjoy reading (what I assume to be) accurate and realistic legal jargon, but I'm not one of them. From what I could tell, the entire thing could be summed up as "Man reveals Foundation front, Foundation is mean to man, man sues Foundation."

Seems well written and I was interested up until that point, so maybe I'll come back later and hope I don't miss too much by skipping the legal doc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it fit the story quite well. It gave an artistic verisimilitude to an otherwise convincing narrative, to alter Gilbert's piquant phrase.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think there's a wasted word

This is great news, because I was pretty on-the-fence about how that word-for-word lawsuit document would be received.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This got more enjoyable with every collapsible. I read through the whole thing, then went back and read it through again. I don't think there's a wasted word. Good work.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
The jig is up, the news is out
They finally found me!
The renegade who had it made
Retrieved for a bounty!
Never more to go astray
This'll be the end today
Of the wanted man!

I can't stop writing Broken Masquerade. It's taking over my life. I also want to give a shoutout to this anon on Tumblr a few weeks ago who gave me the summary of my Foundation that I'd never been able to articulate. I wouldn't not have been able to write this properly without the chain of thought those messages inspired, so thanks, anon.

This is also a Doomsday Contest entry for Team Fuhgeddaboutit.

Thank you to AbsentmindedNihilist, Sly161, Rimple, and WrongJohnSilver for feedback. Carolina Beach coast image edited by me and source is Wikimedia Commons here. All other images created by me.
